The Landauer Limit and Reversible Computing

By Jane Researcher, March 2026

Rolf Landauer argued in 1961 that any logically irreversible manipulation of information, such as the erasure of a bit, must be accompanied by a corresponding entropy increase in non-information-bearing degrees of freedom of the information processing apparatus or its environment.

The minimum energy required to erase one bit at temperature T is k_B T ln 2, where k_B is the Boltzmann constant. At room temperature this evaluates to roughly 2.9 zeptojoules per bit, a figure that subsequent experiments have approached but not crossed.

In 2012, Berut and colleagues experimentally verified the Landauer principle by measuring the heat dissipated during a logically irreversible memory erasure procedure on a colloidal particle in a double-well optical trap. Their results showed that the average dissipated heat approaches the Landauer limit in the limit of slow erasure.

This bound has practical implications for the limits of irreversible computation. It does not, however, constrain reversible computation, which can in principle perform logical operations without thermodynamic cost.
